sound_type: overlapping mew + alarm chorus -- colony soundscape of active nesting site; hundreds of birds calling simultaneously; mewing contact calls, alarm waves, long calls, chick begging; emergent colonial aggregate
ecosystem: Colony -- rocky coastal islands, exposed headlands, rooftops, pier structures; most common gull in Pacific Northwest; obligate colonial nester
season: Summer (breeding season May-August; colony acoustically peak during hatching and chick-rearing)
conservation_status: Least Concern (IUCN). North American breeding population ~300,000-400,000. Some decline at monitored Washington State colonies associated with Bald Eagle predation, habitat disturbance, food availability. Remains abundant.
milestone: FIRST LARIDAE entry at degree 289. Gull and tern family enters SPS engine for the first time. Glaucous-winged Gull (Larus glaucescens, Naumann 1840) is the most common, most widespread, most ecologically central gull in the Pacific Northwest. Colony soundscape introduces new acoustic category: emergent colonial sound arising from hundreds of individuals simultaneously.

coordinates.description: Salish Sea and Puget Sound region. Colony sites: Protection Island (Jefferson County WA), Smith and Minor Islands, San Juan archipelago, outer coast rocks and islets. Year-round presence throughout PNW waterfront, ferry terminals, fishing harbors.

acoustic_data.vocalization_type: Colony soundscape -- emergent aggregate of hundreds of simultaneous individual calls. Not a single call type but the superposition of mewing chorus, long calls, alarm waves, and chick begging.
acoustic_data.primary_call: Mew call: 'keeow' or 'aaah-aaah-aaah'. Drawn-out contact and alarm call. Fundamental frequency approximately 800-1,500 Hz. Most frequent vocalization.
acoustic_data.long_call: Head-back posture, series of loud descending laughing notes. Territorial and social contexts. Punctuates background mewing chorus.
acoustic_data.alarm_call: Rapid 'kek-kek-kek' or 'ga-ga-ga'. Approximately 1,500-3,500 Hz. Propagates through colony in seconds as one bird triggers adjacent birds.
acoustic_data.chick_vocalizations: Rapid high-pitched begging calls from hatching through fledging. Adds distinct pitch register to adult vocalization mix. Late June through August.
acoustic_data.frequency_range: 500-4,000 Hz overall. Mew 800-1,500 Hz predominant. Alarm calls 1,500-3,500 Hz. All vocalizations well within human hearing.
acoustic_data.e6_calibration: E=6: sustained multi-layered colonial ambient chorus. Not single explosive event (E=8-9). The background soundscape of an active colony -- continuous, layered, loud but not shocking. Colony scale amplifies individual calls into collective ambient.

nodes.title: First Laridae -- Gull and Tern Family Enters Engine
nodes.description: Degree 289 is the first Laridae entry in the SPS engine. Family includes gulls, terns, skimmers, skuas. Laridae arc: 289 Glaucous-winged Gull colony, 290 Herring Gull flock alarm, 291 Western Gull colony, 292 Common Tern colony. Four ecological contexts: colonial nest site, winter beach flock, rocky island colony, sandy spit colony. Glaucous-winged Gull is the PNW anchor species -- the gull against which all others are measured.

nodes.title: Emergent Colonial Soundscape -- New Acoustic Category
nodes.description: The Glaucous-winged Gull colony soundscape introduces an acoustic category not previously represented in the SPS engine: emergent colonial sound. The colony sound does not originate from any individual bird but arises from hundreds of simultaneous callers. Distinct from individual-scale (single orca, single whale) or small-group (pod) sounds. Colonial soundscape is what hundreds of individuals produce together -- a true acoustic emergent property.

nodes.title: Colonial Nesting -- The Behavioral and Acoustic System
nodes.description: Obligate colonial nester. Colonial nesting advantages: collective predator detection (alarm propagation scales with colony size), information transfer about food sources, physical deterrence of multiple defending adults. Colony sites: Protection Island, Smith and Minor Islands, San Juan archipelago, rooftops. Breeding chronology April-August; peak acoustic activity June-July during hatching.

nodes.title: Glaucous-winged x Western Gull Hybrid Zone
nodes.description: Larus glaucescens hybridizes extensively with Western Gull (Larus occidentalis) in the Washington State overlap zone. Hybrids common throughout Puget Sound and Salish Sea. One of the most-studied gull hybrid zones in North American ornithology. Intermediate phenotypes challenge field identification. Context for the Western Gull entry at SPS 291.

cross_references.relationship: E=9 mathcore (Tacoma) + E=6 colonial chorus = E=15. Botch's mathematical precision in irregular time signatures vs. the organic emergent chaos of hundreds of birds calling simultaneously. Both are complex sound events. One composed and intentional; one ecological and emergent. E=9 arc closes here.
Second Laridae entry. E rises from 6 to 7. Winter beach flock vs. summer colony. Alarm chorus vs. ambient colonial mewing. Raucous vs. layered.
Third Laridae entry. E=7. Rocky island Pacific coast specialist. Second colony soundscape. The hybrid partner species -- Glaucous-winged x Western hybrids documented throughout Puget Sound.
Fourth Laridae entry. E=7. Sandy spit nesting. Tern sub-family's distinct acoustic character: sharp, higher-pitched keerr colony screaming. Arc closes.
Phalacrocoracidae E=5 colonial nesting waterbird precedes first Laridae. Both colonial. Both summer. Different family, same nesting strategy.
